title: Shirazi Salad

overview.description: A finely diced Persian salad of cucumber, tomato, red onion and herbs dressed with sharp citrus and olive oil.

ingredients: 1 small handful fresh flat-leaf parsley, finely chopped
1 small handful fresh mint leaves, finely chopped
½ tsp salt, or to taste
method: Place the vegetables in a large bowl.
Add the chopped parsley and mint.
Add the lime juice, olive oil, salt and black pepper.
Taste and adjust the acidity and seasoning before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Shirazi salad is named after Shiraz in southern Iran. Many traditional versions use ab-ghooreh, or verjuice, which provides a distinctive sharp acidity.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How finely diced vegetables create a different salad experience from salads built around large chunks, and how acidity can be the defining flavour of a dish.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A very simple, inexpensive and refreshing salad that provides a sharp, acidic contrast to the richer and more substantial salads already in the collection.
chapman-tips: The vegetables should be diced fairly finely and consistently.
Use ripe tomatoes but avoid allowing the salad to become excessively watery.
Traditional versions are often very acidic; don't be afraid of a noticeable sharpness.
Verjuice is worth trying if you ever come across it.
Add the dressing shortly before serving if you want the vegetables to remain crisp.
Fresh mint is particularly important to the character of the salad.
This salad works very well alongside rice dishes and grilled foods.
A little sumac can be added, although it is not essential to the classic version.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The vegetables release water as they stand.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten the same day. Leftovers can be drained and used as a filling for wraps or served with leftover rice.
